questions.prompt: Set of all valid input values (x-values) for a function.
questions.explanation: Domain is the set of all x-values for which f(x) is defined, a core F-IF.1 idea on the NY Algebra 2 Regents. Restrictions arise from: (1) division-by-zero in rational functions (e.g., f(x)=1/(x-3) has domain x≠3), (2) negative radicands under even roots (e.g., g(x)=√(x-5) has domain x≥5), and (3) non-positive arguments of logarithms (e.g., h(x)=log₂(x+1) has domain x>-1). Regents items frequently ask students to express the domain in interval or set-builder notation, such as (-∞,3)∪(3,∞) or {x|x≠3}. Always state restrictions explicitly when simplifying rational expressions because the simplified form may hide them.

questions.prompt: Set of all output values (y-values) a function can produce.
questions.explanation: Range is the complete collection of y-values produced by a function (F-IF.1). For f(x)=x² the range is y≥0; for g(x)=2^x the range is y>0; for h(x)=sin x the range is -1≤y≤1. Determining range often requires identifying the vertex of a parabola, the horizontal asymptote of an exponential, the amplitude/midline of a sinusoid, or the bounds of a square-root function (e.g., y=√x+3 has range y≥3). On the Algebra 2 Regents, range questions appear in F-IF.4-7 modeling items asking students to interpret what y-values are possible given a real-world context such as population, profit, or temperature.

questions.prompt: Notation like f(x) that names a function and its input variable.
questions.explanation: Function notation, written f(x), names the dependent variable produced by input x (F-IF.2). Leonhard Euler introduced this notation in 1734. To evaluate f(x)=x²-4x+3 at x=2: f(2)=(2)²-4(2)+3=4-8+3=-1. Composite functions chain notations: (f∘g)(x)=f(g(x)). The Algebra 2 Regents tests this through symbolic substitution (e.g., find f(x+h)), reading values off graphs (find f(3)), evaluating composite functions, and identifying f(x)=g(x) graphically as intersection points. Misreading f(x+2) as f(x)+2 is a common error worth flagging in class review.

questions.prompt: Function where every output corresponds to exactly one input, passing the horizontal line test.
questions.explanation: A one-to-one function pairs distinct inputs with distinct outputs, so no horizontal line crosses its graph more than once (F-BF.4). One-to-one functions are exactly those that have inverse functions that are themselves functions. f(x)=x³ is one-to-one (passes horizontal line test); f(x)=x² is not, because f(2)=f(-2)=4. To make x² invertible we restrict its domain to x≥0, giving the inverse √x. On the Regents, one-to-one questions appear in inverse-function items (find f⁻¹, state any domain restriction required) and in interpretation tasks where students must explain why a function cannot be inverted globally.

questions.prompt: Function f⁻¹ that reverses the action of f, so f(f⁻¹(x))=x.
questions.explanation: An inverse function f⁻¹ undoes f: if f(a)=b then f⁻¹(b)=a (F-BF.4). To find an inverse algebraically: (1) write y=f(x), (2) swap x and y, (3) solve for y. Example: for f(x)=2x+5, swap to get x=2y+5, solve y=(x-5)/2, so f⁻¹(x)=(x-5)/2. Graphically, f and f⁻¹ are reflections across the line y=x. Domain of f equals range of f⁻¹ and vice versa. The Algebra 2 Regents tests inverses for linear, exponential (inverse is log), and certain quadratics with restricted domains. Always state domain restrictions when inverting non-one-to-one functions.

questions.prompt: Transformation y=f(x)+k that moves a graph up or down by k units.
questions.explanation: A vertical shift adds a constant k to the output (F-BF.3). y=f(x)+3 shifts every point up 3 units; y=f(x)-2 shifts down 2. For y=x²+5 the vertex moves from (0,0) to (0,5). Vertical shifts change y-intercept and range but never period or amplitude. Compare: y=sin(x)+2 has midline y=2 (shifted up 2); y=2^x-1 has horizontal asymptote y=-1. On the Regents, students must distinguish vertical shifts (outside the function, change to y) from horizontal shifts (inside the function, change to x). Misidentifying which axis a transformation affects is a recurring error in F-BF.3 multiple choice items.

questions.prompt: Transformation y=f(x-h) that slides a graph left or right by h units.
questions.explanation: A horizontal shift changes the input by -h: y=f(x-h) shifts right h units when h>0 (F-BF.3). The sign feels counter-intuitive—y=f(x-3) moves right 3, y=f(x+3) moves left 3—because (x-3) returns the original y-value at the input 3 units further right. For y=(x-4)² the vertex is (4,0); for y=√(x+2) the start point is (-2,0). Horizontal shifts change domain restrictions for radicals and logs (e.g., y=log₂(x-1) has domain x>1). Regents items often ask students to write a transformed equation given a graph description such as 'shift y=2^x left 3 and up 1' → y=2^(x+3)+1.

questions.prompt: Transformation y=-f(x) that flips a graph across the x-axis or y=f(-x) across the y-axis.
questions.explanation: A reflection flips the graph across an axis (F-BF.3). y=-f(x) reflects over the x-axis (every y becomes -y); y=f(-x) reflects over the y-axis (every x becomes -x). For f(x)=x²: -f(x)=-x² opens downward (reflection over x-axis); f(-x)=(-x)²=x² is unchanged because x² is even. For f(x)=x³: -f(x)=-x³ and f(-x)=-x³ give the same graph because x³ is odd. The Regents tests this in compositional transformations: y=-(x+2)²-3 is the parent y=x² shifted left 2, reflected over x-axis, shifted down 3, in that order. Sign placement matters.

questions.prompt: Transformation y=f(bx) with |b|>1 that squeezes a graph toward the y-axis.
questions.explanation: A horizontal compression by factor 1/|b| occurs when |b|>1 inside the function (F-BF.3). y=f(2x) squeezes horizontally by factor 1/2: where the parent had y=f(4) at x=4, the compressed graph reaches the same y at x=2. For y=sin(2x) the period becomes 2π/2=π (compressed from 2π). For y=cos(πx/3) the period is 2π/(π/3)=6. The relationship is period=2π/|b|. Regents items frequently embed this in sinusoidal modeling: a tide that completes one cycle every 12 hours has b=2π/12=π/6, so y=A·sin((π/6)t)+D. Distinguishing compression (|b|>1) from horizontal stretch (|b|<1) is a high-frequency error.

questions.prompt: Parent function f(x)=x with slope 1 passing through the origin.
questions.explanation: The linear parent function f(x)=x is the simplest member of the linear family y=mx+b (F-IF.7a). Its slope is 1, y-intercept 0, domain and range are both all real numbers, and average rate of change is constant. All other linear functions are vertical/horizontal shifts and stretches of this parent: y=3x-2 is y=x stretched vertically by 3 and shifted down 2. Linear models on the Algebra 2 Regents appear in residual-analysis items and in contrast with exponential models (linear adds the same amount per step; exponential multiplies by the same factor). Recognizing linear behavior in a table means checking that consecutive differences are equal.

questions.prompt: Parent function f(x)=x² with vertex at the origin and U-shape opening up.
questions.explanation: The quadratic parent function f(x)=x² is the foundational parabola (F-IF.7a). Vertex (0,0), axis of symmetry x=0, opens upward, range y≥0. All quadratics y=a(x-h)²+k are stretches, reflections, and shifts of this parent. Key features include zeros (x=0 with multiplicity 2), even symmetry (f(-x)=f(x)), and constant second differences. On the Algebra 2 Regents, the quadratic appears as a stepping stone to higher polynomials and in modeling projectile motion. Quadratic-formula items expect students to compute the discriminant b²-4ac to count real roots: positive→two real, zero→one repeated, negative→two complex conjugates.

questions.prompt: V-shaped parent function f(x)=|x| with vertex at the origin.
questions.explanation: The absolute value parent function f(x)=|x| produces a V-shape with vertex (0,0), slopes -1 (left) and +1 (right) (F-IF.7b). Domain is all real numbers; range is y≥0. Absolute value equations like |x-3|=5 split into x-3=5 or x-3=-5, giving x=8 or x=-2. Inequalities like |x|<a become -a<x<a (intersection); |x|>a becomes x<-a or x>a (union). On the Algebra 2 Regents, absolute value functions appear in piecewise representations and transformation items: y=2|x-1|-3 has vertex (1,-3), opens upward with slope ±2. The function is not differentiable at the vertex, a fact that distinguishes it from quadratic minima.

questions.prompt: Parent function f(x)=√x starting at the origin and increasing in the first quadrant.
questions.explanation: The square-root parent function f(x)=√x is the inverse of y=x² restricted to x≥0 (F-IF.7b). Domain is x≥0; range is y≥0. The graph begins at (0,0) and increases without bound but with decreasing steepness. Transformations: y=√(x-2)+1 starts at (2,1). To solve √(x+3)=x-3 on the Regents, square both sides giving x+3=(x-3)²=x²-6x+9, leading to x²-7x+6=0, (x-1)(x-6)=0; check both: x=1 fails (√4≠1-3), x=6 works. Always test for extraneous solutions when squaring. The square-root function is one of the named parent functions in F-IF.7.

questions.prompt: Parent function f(x)=b^x with b>1 showing rapid growth and horizontal asymptote y=0.
questions.explanation: The exponential parent function f(x)=b^x (b>1) has domain all reals, range y>0, horizontal asymptote y=0, and y-intercept (0,1) (F-IF.7e). It grows by a constant factor per unit increase in x, distinguishing it from polynomial growth. Common bases: 2 (computer science), e≈2.71828 (natural growth, continuous compounding), and 10 (orders of magnitude). The inverse of y=b^x is y=log_b(x). On the Algebra 2 Regents, exponential models appear in compound-interest items A=P(1+r/n)^(nt), continuous growth A=Pe^(rt), and half-life problems N(t)=N₀(1/2)^(t/h). When 0<b<1 the function decays; when b>1 it grows. Solving b^x=k uses logarithms: x=log_b(k).

questions.prompt: Operation (f∘g)(x)=f(g(x)) that applies g first, then f.
questions.explanation: Function composition feeds g's output into f as input (F-BF.1). If f(x)=2x+1 and g(x)=x², then (f∘g)(x)=f(x²)=2x²+1, but (g∘f)(x)=g(2x+1)=(2x+1)²=4x²+4x+1. Composition is not commutative. The domain of f∘g requires both that x is in the domain of g and g(x) is in the domain of f. Example: if g(x)=√x and f(x)=1/x, then (f∘g)(x)=1/√x with domain x>0. On the Algebra 2 Regents, composition shows up in symbolic evaluation, real-world chains (cost→tax→tip), and verifying inverses (f(f⁻¹(x))=x).

questions.prompt: Result f(f⁻¹(x))=x showing that a function and its inverse undo each other.
questions.explanation: Inverse composition gives the identity: f(f⁻¹(x))=x and f⁻¹(f(x))=x when each is defined (F-BF.4). This is the formal definition that establishes inverses on the Regents. Example: for f(x)=3x-7, f⁻¹(x)=(x+7)/3; check f(f⁻¹(x))=3·(x+7)/3-7=x+7-7=x. Inverse composition is a quick verification: students who derive an inverse can confirm by composing in either order and checking that the result simplifies to x. Failing this check means an algebra error in the inversion process. For exponential and logarithmic pairs: 2^(log₂ x)=x for x>0, and log₂(2^x)=x for all x.

questions.prompt: Function defined by different rules on different intervals of its domain.
questions.explanation: A piecewise function uses multiple formulas, each on a specific interval (F-IF.7b). Example: f(x)= {x² if x<0; 2x+1 if x≥0}. Evaluate by checking which interval contains the input: f(-3)=(-3)²=9; f(2)=2(2)+1=5. Piecewise graphs show explicit break points; open or closed circles indicate strict/non-strict inequalities at boundaries. The absolute value function f(x)=|x| is the piecewise f(x)={-x if x<0; x if x≥0}. On the Algebra 2 Regents, piecewise problems appear in tax-bracket modeling, step functions, and graph-interpretation items where students must read off values from a multi-segment graph.

questions.prompt: Set of x-values intentionally cut down so that a function becomes one-to-one or defined.
questions.explanation: A restricted domain limits inputs to make a function invertible or to match a real-world context (F-BF.4). The classic example: y=x² is not one-to-one, but restricting to x≥0 makes y=x² invertible, with inverse y=√x. Inverse trig functions also require domain restrictions: sin⁻¹ has domain [-1,1] and range [-π/2,π/2]. On the Regents, real-world restrictions appear constantly: time t≥0, population n≥0 (and often integer), height ≥0. Even when an algebraic equation is defined for all reals, the domain in context may be restricted; questions often ask students to identify a 'reasonable domain' for the model.

questions.prompt: Quantity [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a) measuring how fast a function changes on an interval.
questions.explanation: Average rate of change is the slope of the secant line from (a,f(a)) to (b,f(b)), computed as [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a) (F-IF.6). For f(x)=x² on [1,4]: [f(4)-f(1)]/(4-1)=(16-1)/3=5. This is the Algebra 2 generalization of slope: for linear functions it equals the (constant) slope; for non-linear functions it varies by interval. On the Algebra 2 Regents, students compare average rates of change across function families to argue linear vs exponential behavior, or to interpret average growth/decay in real contexts. Be careful: 'average rate of change' is not the instantaneous rate (that's calculus); it is a two-point computation.

questions.prompt: Notation like (-3, 5] or (2, ∞) used to describe sets of real numbers.
questions.explanation: Interval notation describes a contiguous set of real numbers using parentheses for strict inequalities and brackets for inclusive (F-IF.5). [2,7] means 2≤x≤7; (-∞,3) means x<3; (1,4]∪[6,∞) means 1<x≤4 or x≥6. Always use parentheses with ±∞. On the Algebra 2 Regents, interval notation appears in domain/range answers, increasing/decreasing interval questions, and solution sets of inequalities. A function is increasing on (a,b) if f(x₁)<f(x₂) for all x₁<x₂ in (a,b). Note that we usually write open intervals for increasing/decreasing analysis because turning points are excluded.

questions.prompt: Point on a graph lower than all nearby points, forming a valley.
questions.explanation: A relative (local) minimum is a point where f(c)≤f(x) for all x in some open interval around c (F-IF.4)—a valley on the graph. For f(x)=x³-12x: f'(x)=3x²-12=0 gives x=±2, with relative min at x=2 (value -16) and relative max at x=-2 (value 16). On the Algebra 2 Regents (which does not require calculus), students typically identify minima from a graph or from technology, then state both the x-coordinate (where) and the y-coordinate (minimum value). Be precise: the minimum value is the y-coordinate; the location is the x-coordinate. Confusing these is a common Regents error.

questions.prompt: Description of what f(x) does as x→∞ and x→-∞.
questions.explanation: End behavior describes the limit of f(x) as x approaches ±∞ (F-IF.7c). For polynomials it is governed by the leading term ax^n: if n is even and a>0, both ends rise (as x→±∞, f(x)→+∞); if n is even and a<0, both ends fall; if n is odd and a>0, left falls and right rises; if n is odd and a<0, left rises and right falls. For exponentials y=2^x: as x→∞, y→∞; as x→-∞, y→0. On the Algebra 2 Regents, end behavior is a high-frequency multiple choice topic; students should sketch the leading-term shape to predict tail directions. Use limit-style notation: f(x)→∞ as x→∞.

questions.prompt: Input value x where f(x)=0, also called an x-intercept or root.
questions.explanation: A zero of a function is a value of x where f(x)=0 (A-APR.3, A-REI.4). Synonyms include root and x-intercept. For f(x)=x²-5x+6=(x-2)(x-3), the zeros are x=2 and x=3. The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra guarantees that a polynomial of degree n has exactly n complex zeros, counting multiplicities. On the Algebra 2 Regents, zeros are found by factoring, applying the quadratic formula x=(-b±√(b²-4ac))/(2a), graphing technology, the rational root theorem, or synthetic division. Complex zeros of polynomials with real coefficients come in conjugate pairs: if 3+2i is a zero, then 3-2i is also a zero.

questions.prompt: Highest exponent in a polynomial, like the 4 in 3x⁴-2x+1.
questions.explanation: The degree of a polynomial is the highest exponent on the variable (A-APR.1). For f(x)=3x⁴-2x+1, degree=4. Degree controls end behavior, the maximum number of real zeros (at most n for degree n), and the maximum number of turning points (at most n-1). Quadratic: degree 2, up to 2 zeros, 1 turning point. Cubic: degree 3, up to 3 zeros, 2 turning points. Quartic: degree 4, up to 4 zeros, 3 turning points. Constant terms have degree 0; the zero polynomial has undefined degree. On the Algebra 2 Regents, degree appears in classifying polynomials, predicting end behavior, and applying the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra: a polynomial of degree n has exactly n complex zeros counting multiplicities.

questions.prompt: Number in front of the highest-degree term, like the 3 in 3x⁴-2x+1.
questions.explanation: The leading coefficient is the constant multiplying the highest-power term (A-APR.1). Together with degree it determines end behavior: for an=3 (positive), even degree gives both ends up, odd degree gives left down/right up; for an<0 the behavior reverses. The leading coefficient also affects the steepness of the graph for large |x|: |an|>1 produces a steeper rise/fall than the basic x^n. On the Algebra 2 Regents, students use leading coefficient and degree together to choose the correct end-behavior description in multiple choice items. In standard form an x^n + ... + a₁x + a₀, only the leading term controls behavior at ±∞, regardless of middle terms.

questions.prompt: Term in a polynomial with no variable, like the 5 in 2x³-x+5; also the y-intercept value.
questions.explanation: The constant term is a₀ in standard form, the value of the polynomial when x=0, so it equals the y-intercept (A-APR.1, F-IF.7c). For f(x)=2x³-x+5, the constant is 5 and the y-intercept is (0,5). The constant term is also the product of all roots times (-1)^n divided by the leading coefficient (from Vieta's formulas). On the Algebra 2 Regents, students locate the y-intercept quickly by reading the constant term, useful when comparing transformations or interpreting models. In factored form f(x)=a(x-r₁)(x-r₂)...(x-r_n), the constant equals a·(-r₁)(-r₂)...(-r_n)=a·(-1)^n·∏r_i.

questions.prompt: Behavior of f(x) as x approaches positive or negative infinity, determined by degree and leading coefficient.
questions.explanation: End behavior describes f(x) as x→±∞ (F-IF.7c). The rules: (1) even degree, positive leading coefficient: f(x)→+∞ as x→±∞ (both ends up, like x²); (2) even degree, negative leading coefficient: both ends down (like -x²); (3) odd degree, positive leading coefficient: left end down, right end up (like x³); (4) odd degree, negative leading coefficient: left up, right down (like -x³). On the Algebra 2 Regents, end behavior is a frequent multiple choice topic; students should ignore the lower-degree terms and focus on the leading term's sign and exponent parity. Notation: f(x)→∞ as x→∞.

questions.prompt: Value of x where a polynomial equals zero; also called a root or x-intercept.
questions.explanation: A zero (root, x-intercept) is a value of x for which f(x)=0 (A-APR.3, A-REI.4). To find zeros, factor or apply the quadratic formula for degree 2, or use synthetic division/rational root theorem for higher degrees. Example: f(x)=x³-7x+6 factors as (x-1)(x+3)(x-2), with zeros 1, -3, 2. By the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra a polynomial of degree n has exactly n complex zeros counting multiplicity. Real zeros are where the graph crosses or touches the x-axis. On the Algebra 2 Regents, finding zeros is one of the most-tested skills: factoring, completing the square, the quadratic formula, and synthetic division all appear.

questions.prompt: Number of times a factor (x-r) repeats in a polynomial, controlling crossing vs. touching behavior at x=r.
questions.explanation: Multiplicity is the exponent of a repeated factor (A-APR.3). For f(x)=(x-2)²(x+1)³, zero x=2 has multiplicity 2 and zero x=-1 has multiplicity 3. Odd multiplicity: graph crosses x-axis at the zero (with a flatter cross for higher odd multiplicities). Even multiplicity: graph touches and bounces off the x-axis. Example: f(x)=x²(x-1) has a touch at x=0 (mult. 2) and a cross at x=1 (mult. 1). Total of all multiplicities equals the degree. On the Algebra 2 Regents, multiplicity controls how the graph behaves at each zero, and is a common topic in graph-matching and graph-sketching items.

questions.prompt: Theorem stating (x-r) is a factor of polynomial P(x) if and only if P(r)=0.
questions.explanation: The Factor Theorem links zeros and factors: (x-r) is a factor of P(x) if and only if P(r)=0 (A-APR.2). To check whether (x-3) is a factor of P(x)=x³-7x+6, compute P(3)=27-21+6=12; since P(3)≠0, (x-3) is not a factor. Compute P(1)=1-7+6=0, so (x-1) is a factor. After identifying one factor, use synthetic division to reduce to a lower-degree polynomial: x³-7x+6=(x-1)(x²+x-6)=(x-1)(x+3)(x-2). On the Algebra 2 Regents, the Factor Theorem is the basis for factoring cubics and quartics that don't factor by inspection, and for proving (x-r) is a factor without performing full division.

questions.prompt: Theorem stating the remainder when P(x) is divided by (x-r) equals P(r).
questions.explanation: The Remainder Theorem says: when P(x) is divided by (x-r), the remainder equals P(r) (A-APR.2). This is a quick way to evaluate polynomials without substitution—especially useful with synthetic division. Example: P(x)=x⁴-3x²+5; to find the remainder when divided by (x-2), compute P(2)=16-12+5=9, so remainder is 9. This implies the Factor Theorem as a special case: if P(r)=0 then the remainder is 0 and (x-r) divides P(x) evenly. On the Algebra 2 Regents, the Remainder Theorem appears in items asking for the remainder of a division, or as the justification for the Factor Theorem in constructed-response items where students must explain why (x-r) is a factor.

questions.prompt: Zero of the form a+bi (b≠0) that must appear with its conjugate a-bi in real-coefficient polynomials.
questions.explanation: A complex root has the form a+bi with imaginary part b≠0 (N-CN.7). The Conjugate Pairs Theorem: if a polynomial has real coefficients and a+bi is a root, then a-bi is also a root. Example: if P(x)=x²-4x+13 has root 2+3i, then 2-3i is also a root, and P(x)=(x-(2+3i))(x-(2-3i))=x²-4x+13. Use the quadratic formula to find complex roots when the discriminant is negative: for x²+2x+5=0, x=(-2±√(-16))/2=-1±2i. On the Algebra 2 Regents, complex roots appear in solving quadratics with negative discriminant and in higher-degree polynomials where students count real vs complex zeros and use Vieta's relations.

questions.prompt: Process of dividing one polynomial by another to find quotient and remainder.
questions.explanation: Polynomial division divides P(x) by D(x) to write P(x)=D(x)Q(x)+R(x), where deg R < deg D (A-APR.6). For degree-1 divisors, synthetic division is fastest; for higher-degree divisors, long division is the method. Example: divide x³-2x²+5 by (x-3): synthetic with root 3 gives quotient x²+x+3 remainder 14, so x³-2x²+5=(x-3)(x²+x+3)+14. The result lets students rewrite improper rational expressions: (x³-2x²+5)/(x-3)=x²+x+3+14/(x-3). On the Algebra 2 Regents, polynomial division underpins factoring higher-degree polynomials and analyzing rational functions for slant asymptotes.

questions.prompt: Shortcut technique using only coefficients to divide a polynomial by (x-r).
questions.explanation: Synthetic division is a streamlined method for dividing by a linear factor (x-r) using only coefficients (A-APR.6). Steps: write coefficients in a row (use 0 for missing terms), bring down the leading coefficient, multiply by r and add to the next coefficient, repeat. The final value is the remainder; the others are the coefficients of the quotient. Example: x³-4x+1 ÷ (x-2): coefficients 1, 0, -4, 1; bring down 1; 1·2=2, 0+2=2; 2·2=4, -4+4=0; 0·2=0, 1+0=1. Quotient: x²+2x+0, remainder 1. So x³-4x+1=(x-2)(x²+2x)+1. On the Regents, synthetic division pairs with the Rational Root Theorem to factor cubics and quartics.

questions.prompt: Standard algorithm for dividing polynomials that mirrors numerical long division.
questions.explanation: Polynomial long division extends numerical long division to any divisor (A-APR.6). Unlike synthetic division, it works for divisors of any degree. Example: divide x³+2x²-x+5 by (x²-1). Step 1: x³÷x²=x; multiply x(x²-1)=x³-x; subtract to get 2x²+5. Step 2: 2x²÷x²=2; multiply 2(x²-1)=2x²-2; subtract to get 7. Quotient x+2, remainder 7. Write: x³+2x²-x+5=(x²-1)(x+2)+7. On the Algebra 2 Regents, long division is required when dividing by quadratic or higher-degree polynomials and when finding slant or curve asymptotes of rational functions where deg(num)-deg(den)≥1.

questions.prompt: Combine two polynomials term by term to get a new polynomial of the same or lower degree.
questions.explanation: The sum of polynomials adds coefficients of like-degree terms (A-APR.1). (3x³-2x+4)+(x³+5x²-1)=4x³+5x²-2x+3. Subtraction works the same way: distribute the negative. The degree of the sum is at most the larger of the two degrees, and equals it when the leading terms don't cancel. If they cancel (e.g., (x³-5)+(-x³+2x)=2x-5), the degree drops. Polynomial sums form a closed set under addition: the sum of polynomials is always a polynomial. On the Algebra 2 Regents, polynomial addition appears in simplifying complex expressions and in checking that two algebraically different expressions are equivalent.

questions.prompt: Result of multiplying polynomials by distributing every term of one across every term of the other.
questions.explanation: Polynomial multiplication distributes each term of the first factor across every term of the second (A-APR.1). (x+3)(x²-2x+5)=x³-2x²+5x+3x²-6x+15=x³+x²-x+15. The degree of the product equals the sum of the degrees of the factors. Useful identities: (a+b)²=a²+2ab+b²; (a-b)²=a²-2ab+b²; (a+b)(a-b)=a²-b²; (a+b)³=a³+3a²b+3ab²+b³. The Binomial Theorem generalizes (a+b)^n using binomial coefficients. On the Algebra 2 Regents, polynomial products appear in expanding factored forms, in the Binomial Theorem, and in verifying algebraic identities through expansion.

questions.prompt: Point where a graph crosses the y-axis, found by evaluating f(0).
questions.explanation: The y-intercept is the point (0,f(0)) where the graph crosses the y-axis (F-IF.7c). A function has at most one y-intercept (else it wouldn't be a function). For a polynomial f(x)=an x^n+...+a₁x+a₀, the y-intercept is (0,a₀), which is just the constant term. Example: f(x)=2x³-x²+5 has y-intercept (0,5). For other function families: exponential y=b^x has y-intercept (0,1); y=a·b^x has (0,a). On the Algebra 2 Regents, the y-intercept is a quick check on graph-matching items: substitute x=0 to see which graph crosses the y-axis at the correct value, eliminating wrong choices fast.

questions.prompt: Highest or lowest value of a polynomial on a small interval, at a turning point of the graph.
questions.explanation: A local (relative) extremum is the largest or smallest function value in a small neighborhood (F-IF.4). For polynomials these occur at turning points where the graph changes direction. A degree-n polynomial has at most n-1 local extrema. Example: f(x)=x³-3x has local max at x=-1 (value 2) and local min at x=1 (value -2); these are not the global max/min because the cubic extends to ±∞. On the Algebra 2 Regents, students locate local extrema from graphs or technology, distinguish them from global extrema, and use them to argue increasing/decreasing intervals. Local extremum locations are sometimes called critical points (a calculus term that appears informally in Algebra 2).

questions.prompt: Polynomial graph type whose two ends rise (or both fall), characterized by an even highest exponent.
questions.explanation: An even-degree polynomial has matching end behavior: both ends up (if leading coefficient is positive) or both ends down (if negative) (F-IF.7c). Examples: y=x² (both ends up), y=-x⁴ (both ends down), y=2x⁶-3x²+1 (both ends up). The graph resembles a U or inverted U at extreme x-values. Even-degree polynomials must have a global maximum (negative leading coefficient) or a global minimum (positive), unlike odd-degree polynomials. On the Algebra 2 Regents, recognizing even-degree end behavior helps eliminate wrong answer choices in graph-matching problems: any graph whose tails go in opposite directions cannot represent an even-degree polynomial.

questions.prompt: Polynomial graph type whose left end falls while the right rises (or vice versa), with an odd highest exponent.
questions.explanation: An odd-degree polynomial has opposite end behaviors: one end up, one end down (F-IF.7c). With positive leading coefficient: left end falls, right end rises (like x³, x⁵). With negative leading coefficient: left end rises, right end falls (like -x³). The graph crosses the x-axis at least once by the Intermediate Value Theorem, since values transition from -∞ to +∞ (or vice versa). Odd-degree polynomials therefore have at least one real zero. On the Algebra 2 Regents, odd-degree end behavior appears in graph-matching items and in arguments that every cubic crosses the x-axis at least once. Examples include y=x³-x (three crosses) and y=x³+x (one cross, two complex roots).

questions.prompt: Equivalent expression of a polynomial written as a product of linear and/or quadratic factors.
questions.explanation: Factored form expresses a polynomial as a product, often a(x-r₁)(x-r₂)...(x-r_n), revealing zeros directly (A-SSE.2, A-APR.3). For x³-7x+6, factored form (x-1)(x-2)(x+3) shows zeros at 1, 2, -3. Factoring techniques: GCF, difference of squares (a²-b²=(a+b)(a-b)), sum/difference of cubes, grouping, quadratic formula. For quadratics x²+bx+c look for two numbers that multiply to c and add to b. On the Algebra 2 Regents, converting between factored and standard form is a frequent task; factored form is the right tool when asked about zeros, x-intercepts, or sign of f(x), while standard form is needed for the y-intercept and standard polynomial arithmetic.

questions.prompt: Polynomial form written as an x^n + ... + a₁x + a₀ with decreasing exponents.
questions.explanation: Standard form arranges terms by decreasing degree: a_n x^n + a_(n-1) x^(n-1) + ... + a₁x + a₀ (A-APR.1). For example, 3x³-5x²+0x+7 in standard form. Standard form makes degree, leading coefficient, and constant (y-intercept) immediately visible. It is the canonical form for polynomial division, synthetic division, and substitution of values. Convert from factored form by multiplying out. On the Algebra 2 Regents, standard form is required when applying the Rational Root Theorem, performing synthetic division, identifying coefficients for the quadratic formula (a, b, c), or describing end behavior from the leading term. Standard form is also called expanded form or polynomial form.

questions.prompt: Theorem listing all possible rational zeros of a polynomial as ±(factor of constant)/(factor of leading coefficient).
questions.explanation: The Rational Root Theorem: if p/q is a rational zero (in lowest terms) of a polynomial with integer coefficients, then p divides the constant term and q divides the leading coefficient (A-APR.3). Example: for 2x³-3x²-3x+2, p∈{±1,±2} and q∈{±1,±2}, so candidates are ±1, ±2, ±1/2. Test each by synthetic division or direct substitution. Found rational zeros: 2, 1/2, -1. Once one rational zero is found, divide to lower the degree and continue. On the Algebra 2 Regents, the Rational Root Theorem combined with synthetic division is the standard method for factoring cubics and quartics that don't factor by inspection. Irrational and complex zeros are not produced by this theorem.

questions.prompt: Pair of complex zeros a+bi and a-bi that always occur together in polynomials with real coefficients.
questions.explanation: A complex conjugate pair consists of a+bi and a-bi (N-CN.7). For polynomials with real coefficients, non-real zeros must occur in conjugate pairs because complex roots of real-coefficient equations come from quadratic factors x²-2ax+(a²+b²) with negative discriminant. Example: if 2+5i is a zero of P(x), then 2-5i is also a zero, and the quadratic factor is (x-(2+5i))(x-(2-5i))=x²-4x+29. This pairs with the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra: a polynomial of odd degree with real coefficients must have at least one real zero. On the Algebra 2 Regents, conjugate pairs let students reconstruct polynomials from partial root information.

questions.prompt: Degree-2 polynomial factor like x²+1 with no real roots, irreducible over the real numbers.
questions.explanation: An irreducible quadratic factor over the reals is a quadratic with negative discriminant (b²-4ac<0) (A-APR.3). Examples: x²+1, x²+x+1 (discriminant -3), x²+4x+5 (discriminant -4). These factors cannot be split into real linear factors. Every real polynomial factors into a product of real linear factors and irreducible quadratic factors. Example: x⁴+x²-2=(x²-1)(x²+2)=(x-1)(x+1)(x²+2), where x²+2 is irreducible. On the Algebra 2 Regents, recognizing irreducible quadratics helps stop factoring when no further real factors exist, and connects to complex-conjugate root pairs that produce quadratic factors with negative discriminant.

questions.prompt: Algebraic expression of the form P(x)/Q(x) where P and Q are polynomials and Q(x)≠0.
questions.explanation: A rational expression is a ratio of two polynomials (A-APR.7). Examples: (x²-4)/(x+2), (3x+1)/(x²-1). The denominator must be nonzero, so domain excludes any x where Q(x)=0. To simplify, factor numerator and denominator, then cancel common factors—but note any cancellations of factors (x-a) introduce 'holes' (removable discontinuities) in the graph at x=a. Example: (x²-4)/(x+2)=(x-2)(x+2)/(x+2)=x-2 with restriction x≠-2. On the Algebra 2 Regents, rational expression problems test factoring fluency, domain awareness, and the distinction between vertical asymptotes (factor remains in denominator after canceling) and holes (factor canceled).

questions.prompt: Input value that makes a rational expression's denominator zero, removed from the domain.
questions.explanation: An excluded value is an x-value that makes the denominator zero, undefined for the rational expression (A-APR.7). For (x+3)/(x²-9)=(x+3)/((x-3)(x+3)), excluded values are x=3 and x=-3 (even after canceling). Always identify excluded values from the original (unsimplified) expression because simplifying can hide them. The domain of the rational expression is all reals except the excluded values, written {x|x≠3, x≠-3} or (-∞,-3)∪(-3,3)∪(3,∞). On the Algebra 2 Regents, excluded values appear in domain questions and in identifying whether a denominator zero produces a vertical asymptote (still present after simplifying) or a hole (canceled).

questions.prompt: Expression like (x+1)(x-2) shared in two denominators, used when adding or subtracting rational expressions.
questions.explanation: A common denominator is an expression divisible by every denominator in a sum or difference (A-APR.7). To add 1/(x-1)+2/(x+3), multiply each fraction by the missing factor: (x+3)/((x-1)(x+3))+2(x-1)/((x-1)(x+3))=(x+3+2x-2)/((x-1)(x+3))=(3x+1)/((x-1)(x+3)). The Least Common Denominator (LCD) is the product of distinct factors raised to the highest power they appear. For 1/(x²-1)+1/(x²+x), factor: (x-1)(x+1) and x(x+1); LCD = x(x-1)(x+1). On the Algebra 2 Regents, students add and subtract rational expressions in simplification items and in rate-time-work problems leading to rational equations.

questions.prompt: Rational expression whose numerator or denominator itself contains a fraction.
questions.explanation: A complex fraction has fractions in its numerator, denominator, or both (A-APR.7). Example: (1/x+1/y)/(1/x-1/y). Two simplification methods: (1) combine top and bottom into single fractions, then divide: ((y+x)/xy)/((y-x)/xy)=(y+x)/(y-x); (2) multiply numerator and denominator by the LCD of all internal fractions: multiply by xy/xy to get (y+x)/(y-x). On the Algebra 2 Regents, complex fractions appear in items where students simplify before solving an equation or interpret a rational expression in context (e.g., harmonic mean, parallel resistor formula 1/R=1/R₁+1/R₂).

questions.prompt: Final rational expression with no common factors in numerator and denominator and excluded values listed.
questions.explanation: A simplified rational expression has no common factors between numerator and denominator, and includes notation of all excluded values from the original (A-APR.7). Example: (x²-9)/(x²-3x)=(x-3)(x+3)/(x(x-3))=(x+3)/x, with x≠0 and x≠3. The x≠3 restriction is essential even though the (x-3) was canceled—the original was undefined there. On the Algebra 2 Regents, leaving out excluded values is a common point-loss error in simplification items. Always check that the simplified expression is equivalent to the original by selecting a non-excluded value and substituting into both. A graph would show a hole at x=3.

questions.prompt: Line x=a where a rational function's graph approaches ±∞, occurring when the denominator equals zero but the numerator does not.
questions.explanation: A vertical asymptote occurs at x=a when (x-a) is a factor of the denominator but not the numerator (F-IF.7d). For f(x)=(x+2)/(x²-1)=(x+2)/((x-1)(x+1)), vertical asymptotes are at x=1 and x=-1. As x approaches an asymptote, |f(x)|→∞. Determine the direction (±∞) by examining one-sided behavior: for f(x)=1/(x-2), as x→2⁺, f→+∞; as x→2⁻, f→-∞. On the Algebra 2 Regents, vertical asymptotes are a frequent topic in graphing rational functions and in identifying domain restrictions. Always factor before identifying asymptotes—a denominator zero that cancels with a numerator zero produces a hole, not an asymptote.

questions.prompt: Line y=k that a rational function's graph approaches as x→±∞, controlled by degrees of numerator and denominator.
questions.explanation: A horizontal asymptote describes f(x) as x→±∞ (F-IF.7d). Three cases for f(x)=p(x)/q(x): (1) deg p < deg q: horizontal asymptote y=0. (2) deg p = deg q: y=(leading coefficient of p)/(leading coefficient of q). Example: (3x²+1)/(2x²-x) has asymptote y=3/2. (3) deg p > deg q: no horizontal asymptote (there's a slant or curve asymptote instead). On the Algebra 2 Regents, students determine horizontal asymptotes from degree comparisons and use them to sketch end behavior of rational functions. A function may cross its horizontal asymptote at intermediate x-values, unlike vertical asymptotes.

questions.prompt: Diagonal line y=mx+b that a rational function approaches when the numerator's degree is exactly one more than the denominator's.
questions.explanation: A slant (oblique) asymptote arises when deg(numerator) = deg(denominator) + 1 (F-IF.7d). To find it, perform polynomial division: the quotient (ignoring the remainder) is the slant asymptote. Example: f(x)=(x²-3x+2)/(x-1). Long division: x²-3x+2 ÷ (x-1) = x-2 with remainder 0. So f(x)=x-2 with hole at x=1. For (x²+1)/(x), division gives x+1/x; as x→±∞, 1/x→0 and y→x is the slant asymptote. On the Algebra 2 Regents, slant asymptotes appear in graphing problems for cubic-over-quadratic or quadratic-over-linear rational functions; division is the required tool to identify them.

questions.prompt: Single missing point on a rational function's graph where both numerator and denominator share a factor.
questions.explanation: A hole occurs at x=a if (x-a) is a factor of both the numerator and the denominator (F-IF.7d). After canceling, the simplified function evaluates to a specific y-value at x=a, but the original function is undefined there. Example: f(x)=(x²-4)/(x-2)=(x-2)(x+2)/(x-2)=x+2 for x≠2; the graph is the line y=x+2 with a hole at (2,4). To find the y-coordinate of a hole, plug x=a into the simplified expression. On the Algebra 2 Regents, students distinguish holes from vertical asymptotes: a factor that cancels produces a hole, one that does not produces an asymptote.

questions.prompt: Type of discontinuity at a hole, where the limit exists but the function value is undefined.
questions.explanation: A removable discontinuity is a hole that could be 'patched' by defining the function at that point to match the limit (F-IF.7d). For f(x)=(x²-1)/(x-1)=x+1 with x≠1, the limit as x→1 is 2, so defining f(1)=2 removes the discontinuity. Non-removable discontinuities are vertical asymptotes (jump to ∞) and jumps (left and right limits disagree). On the Algebra 2 Regents, the concept of removability links to factoring: if (x-a) cancels, the discontinuity is removable; if (x-a) survives in the denominator, the discontinuity is non-removable (a vertical asymptote). This idea preludes calculus's formal limit definition.

questions.prompt: Equation containing a variable inside a radical, like √(x+3)=5.
questions.explanation: A radical equation contains the variable under a radical (A-REI.2). Solve by isolating the radical, then raising both sides to the index power. Example: √(x+3)=5 → squaring → x+3=25 → x=22. Check: √25=5 ✓. For √(x+5)=x-1: square → x+5=(x-1)²=x²-2x+1 → x²-3x-4=0 → (x-4)(x+1)=0 → x=4 or x=-1. Check x=4: √9=3, 4-1=3 ✓. Check x=-1: √4=2, -1-1=-2 ✗. So x=-1 is extraneous and only x=4 is valid. On the Algebra 2 Regents, every radical equation requires a verification step to weed out extraneous solutions introduced by squaring.

questions.prompt: Non-negative root of a non-negative number, the value returned by the radical symbol √.
questions.explanation: The principal square root is the non-negative root: √25=5, not -5 (N-RN.2). The equation x²=25 has two solutions x=±5, but √25 (the symbol alone) returns only the principal (non-negative) root. Distinguishing these in writing is critical on the Regents: √x is a function returning the non-negative output, whereas ±√x represents both square roots. For even-index radicals (square root, fourth root), only the principal (non-negative) root is returned. For odd-index radicals (cube root, fifth root), there is a unique real root that matches the sign of the radicand. This convention matters when solving equations involving even powers.

questions.prompt: Number n in ⁿ√x telling whether the radical is a square root, cube root, or higher.
questions.explanation: The index of a radical is the small number above the radical symbol, indicating the root: ²√ (square root, often omitted as just √), ³√ (cube root), ⁴√ (fourth root) (N-RN.2). Even index (2,4,6): radicand must be ≥0 in the real numbers; result is non-negative. Odd index (3,5): radicand can be any real; result has same sign as radicand. The relationship to rational exponents: ⁿ√(x^m)=x^(m/n). Example: ³√(x⁶)=x². On the Algebra 2 Regents, students convert between radical and rational-exponent forms, simplify radical expressions, and apply index awareness to set domain restrictions on radical functions.

questions.prompt: Exponent like 1/2 or 2/3 that encodes a radical, with denominator giving the index and numerator the power.
questions.explanation: A rational exponent m/n is defined as ⁿ√(x^m) = (ⁿ√x)^m (N-RN.1). Examples: x^(1/2)=√x, x^(1/3)=³√x, x^(2/3)=³√(x²)=(³√x)². All exponent rules carry over: x^a·x^b=x^(a+b), (x^a)^b=x^(ab), x^a/x^b=x^(a-b). Example: 8^(2/3)=(8^(1/3))²=2²=4. Negative rational exponents reciprocate: x^(-m/n)=1/x^(m/n). On the Algebra 2 Regents, students translate between radical and rational-exponent notation, simplify expressions like (x⁴)^(3/2)=x⁶, and solve equations like x^(2/3)=4 by raising to the reciprocal power: x=4^(3/2)=8.

questions.prompt: Pair like a+b√c and a-b√c whose product is rational, used to rationalize denominators.
questions.explanation: Conjugate pairs are expressions of the form a+b√c and a-b√c, whose product (a+b√c)(a-b√c)=a²-b²c is rational (N-RN.3). Use conjugates to rationalize denominators: 1/(2+√3) · (2-√3)/(2-√3)=(2-√3)/(4-3)=2-√3. Complex conjugate pairs (a+bi and a-bi) work similarly: (a+bi)(a-bi)=a²+b². Use to simplify 1/(3-2i): multiply by (3+2i)/(3+2i)=(3+2i)/(9+4)=(3+2i)/13. On the Algebra 2 Regents, rationalizing denominators and simplifying complex-number expressions both use conjugates. The technique reduces a denominator with a radical or imaginary unit to a real, rational number.

questions.prompt: Apparent solution to an equation that fails when substituted back, often introduced by squaring or multiplying both sides.
questions.explanation: An extraneous solution satisfies a transformed equation but not the original (A-REI.2). Common sources: squaring both sides of a radical equation (turns x=-3 into x²=9, which is satisfied by both ±3), or multiplying both sides by an expression that can be zero. Example: √(x+5)=x-1 squared gives x²-3x-4=0 → x=4 or -1; check shows x=-1 fails. Example: solving 1/(x-1)=x/(x-1) by multiplying both sides by (x-1) gives 1=x, but x=1 was excluded by the original. On the Algebra 2 Regents, checking solutions is non-negotiable after squaring or multiplying by variable expressions; uncaught extraneous solutions cost points.

questions.prompt: Condition on the input variable required for an equation to make sense, like x≥0 in √x.
questions.explanation: A domain restriction limits inputs based on operational needs (F-IF.5). For radical expressions with even index, radicand ≥ 0. For rational expressions, denominator ≠ 0. For logarithms, argument > 0. Real-world restrictions add: time t≥0, count n is a non-negative integer, probability 0≤p≤1. On the Algebra 2 Regents, students state domain restrictions in interval or set-builder notation. When solving equations, the domain narrows the set of acceptable solutions: x²=16 has solutions ±4, but if context requires x≥0, only x=4 is valid. Always check whether algebraic solutions lie within the contextual domain.

questions.prompt: Operation of raising both sides of a radical equation to an even power to eliminate a square root.
questions.explanation: Squaring both sides eliminates square roots but can introduce extraneous solutions (A-REI.2). Procedure: (1) isolate the radical; (2) square both sides; (3) solve the resulting equation; (4) check every candidate in the original equation. Example: √(2x+1)-3=0 → √(2x+1)=3 → 2x+1=9 → x=4. Check √9-3=0 ✓. For two radicals: √(x+4)=√(2x-1)+1; square → x+4=2x-1+2√(2x-1)+1 → 4-x=2√(2x-1); square again, solve, check. Each squaring step can introduce extraneous solutions, so check at the end. On the Algebra 2 Regents, squaring is the standard tool for radical equations.

questions.prompt: Smallest expression divisible by all denominators in a rational equation, used to clear fractions.
questions.explanation: The Least Common Denominator (LCD) is the smallest expression containing each denominator as a factor (A-APR.7). For 1/(x-1)+2/(x²-1)=3: factor x²-1=(x-1)(x+1); LCD=(x-1)(x+1). Multiply every term by the LCD to clear fractions: (x+1)+2=3(x-1)(x+1) → x+3=3x²-3 → 3x²-x-6=0, then solve. Check that solutions don't make any original denominator zero (x≠±1). On the Algebra 2 Regents, the LCD-clearing strategy is the standard method for solving rational equations; alternative is to combine into a single fraction and set the numerator equal to zero. Always check solutions against excluded values.

questions.prompt: Mandatory step of substituting candidate solutions back into the original equation to confirm validity.
questions.explanation: Solution verification is the final, required step for radical and rational equations (A-REI.2). After algebraic manipulation that could introduce extraneous solutions, substitute each candidate into the original equation. Confirm: both sides are defined (no division by zero, no negative radicand), and both sides produce the same numerical value. Example: for √(x-4)+x=10, solving gives x=4 or 16 — but check √0+4=4≠10 (fail), √12+16≈19.5≠10 (fail; need to recheck arithmetic). On the Algebra 2 Regents, verification is worth points: students who simply list final answers from algebra without checking lose credit when an extraneous solution sneaks through. This habit is the explicit content of A-REI.2.

questions.prompt: Relationship y=k/x where two variables change in opposite directions, doubling x halves y.
questions.explanation: Inverse variation has the form y=k/x for a constant k (F-IF.7d). As x increases, y decreases proportionally: if x doubles, y is halved; if x triples, y is divided by 3. Example: pressure × volume = constant (Boyle's Law). To find k, use a known data point: if y=8 when x=3, then k=24 and y=24/x. The graph is a hyperbola with vertical asymptote x=0 and horizontal asymptote y=0 (for k>0, two branches in quadrants I and III). On the Algebra 2 Regents, inverse variation appears as the rational parent function y=1/x and in modeling problems where two quantities have constant product.

questions.prompt: Function of the form y=P(x)/Q(x) where P and Q are polynomials, often featuring asymptotes and holes.
questions.explanation: A rational function is a quotient of polynomials (F-IF.7d). Domain excludes zeros of Q(x). Key features include: vertical asymptotes (where Q(x)=0 and P(x)≠0), horizontal or slant asymptotes (controlled by degrees), holes (where common factors cancel), x-intercepts (where P(x)=0 with Q(x)≠0), and y-intercept (value at x=0, if defined). Example: f(x)=(x-1)/(x²-1)=(x-1)/((x-1)(x+1))=1/(x+1) for x≠1; vertical asymptote x=-1, hole at x=1. On the Algebra 2 Regents, rational functions are a major Unit 3 topic with graph-matching and feature-identification items.

questions.prompt: Continuous piece of a rational or radical graph separated from other pieces by an asymptote or domain gap.
questions.explanation: A branch is a connected piece of a discontinuous graph (F-IF.7d). The function y=1/x has two branches: one in quadrant I (x>0, y>0) and one in quadrant III (x<0, y<0), separated by the asymptotes x=0 and y=0. Rational functions in general have multiple branches separated by vertical asymptotes. For y=1/(x-2)+3, branches are in regions x<2 and x>2, separated by vertical asymptote x=2 and approaching horizontal asymptote y=3. On the Algebra 2 Regents, branch behavior is examined in graphing items and in describing function behavior on different intervals of the domain.

questions.prompt: Set of y-values a function actually produces, often narrower than all reals due to function family limits.
questions.explanation: A restricted range is the actual set of outputs of a function, often a subset of all reals (F-IF.5). y=√x has range y≥0 due to the principal-square-root convention. y=1/x has range y≠0 (the function cannot produce 0). y=sin(x) has range [-1,1]. y=a·b^x+k has range y>k (if a>0) or y<k (if a<0). On the Algebra 2 Regents, range questions require students to think about extrema (for polynomial graphs), asymptotes (for rational and exponential), and natural restrictions (for radicals and trig). Range is computed differently from domain: examine outputs of the function, not inputs. Real-world restrictions (e.g., non-negative quantities) further narrow the range.

questions.prompt: Inverse operation of exponentiation, written log_b(x), answering 'what power do I raise b to to get x?'
questions.explanation: A logarithm is the inverse of exponentiation: log_b(x)=y means b^y=x (F-BF.5). Example: log₂(8)=3 because 2³=8. log₁₀(1000)=3 because 10³=1000. The logarithm function y=log_b(x) is defined only for x>0 and b>0, b≠1. Three laws: log_b(MN)=log_b(M)+log_b(N), log_b(M/N)=log_b(M)-log_b(N), log_b(M^p)=p·log_b(M). The change-of-base formula: log_b(x)=log(x)/log(b)=ln(x)/ln(b). On the Algebra 2 Regents, logarithms appear in solving exponential equations, computing pH and decibels, and inverting exponential functions. The notation log without a subscript usually means log₁₀ (common log); ln means log_e (natural log).

questions.prompt: Relationship between exponential and logarithmic functions: y=b^x and y=log_b(x) reflect across y=x.
questions.explanation: Exponential and logarithmic functions are inverses of each other (F-BF.5). y=b^x and y=log_b(x) are reflections across the line y=x. The exponential has domain all reals, range y>0, horizontal asymptote y=0. The logarithm has domain x>0, range all reals, vertical asymptote x=0. Their composition gives the identity: b^(log_b x)=x for x>0, and log_b(b^x)=x for all x. On the Algebra 2 Regents, this inverse relationship is used to solve exponential equations by 'taking the log of both sides' and logarithmic equations by 'exponentiating both sides.' Example: to solve 2^x=10, take log: x·log 2=log 10, so x=log 10/log 2≈3.32.

questions.prompt: Number b in log_b(x), like the 2 in log₂(8); must be positive and not equal to 1.
questions.explanation: The base of a logarithm is the b in log_b(x) (F-BF.5). Restriction: b>0 and b≠1 (b=1 would make log_1(x) undefined because 1^y=1 for all y). Common bases: 10 (common log, written 'log'), e≈2.71828 (natural log, written 'ln'), and 2 (used in computer science). Changing base uses log_b(x)=log_a(x)/log_a(b) for any valid base a. Example: log₅(12)=log(12)/log(5)≈1.544. On the Algebra 2 Regents, students typically work with log and ln on calculators and use change-of-base to evaluate other-base logarithms. Understanding that 'base' is the bottom number of the logarithm helps avoid confusion with exponent terminology.

questions.prompt: Logarithm with base 10, often written log(x) without explicit base, used for pH and decibels.
questions.explanation: The common logarithm is log base 10, abbreviated log(x) (F-BF.5). log(100)=2, log(1000)=3, log(1)=0, log(0.1)=-1. Used in scientific notation (the characteristic of a number gives its order of magnitude), pH calculations (pH=-log[H⁺]), the Richter earthquake scale, and decibel sound measurement. Solving 10^x=500: x=log(500)≈2.699. The common log key on calculators is usually labeled 'log'. On the Algebra 2 Regents, common log appears in real-world modeling items where data spans many orders of magnitude (sound, earthquakes, chemistry). The common log of a power of 10 is the exponent itself: log(10^n)=n.

questions.prompt: Logarithm with base e≈2.71828, written ln(x), arising in continuous growth models.
questions.explanation: The natural logarithm is log base e≈2.71828, written ln(x) (F-BF.5). Like all logs, ln(1)=0 and ln(e)=1. Used in continuous-growth formula A=Pe^(rt): solving for t gives t=ln(A/P)/r. Inverse pair: ln(e^x)=x and e^(ln x)=x for x>0. Derivative perspective (calculus prep): d/dx[ln x]=1/x. Euler's identity e^(iπ)+1=0 involves e and is sometimes cited in Algebra 2 enrichment for its compactness, but is not on the Regents. The natural log key on calculators is labeled 'ln'. On the Algebra 2 Regents, natural log appears in continuous compounding, radioactive decay, and Newton's law of cooling. The e constant equals lim(n→∞)(1+1/n)^n.

questions.prompt: Law log_b(MN)=log_b(M)+log_b(N) that splits a log of a product into a sum of logs.
questions.explanation: The product property of logs: log_b(MN)=log_b(M)+log_b(N) (A-SSE.2, F-BF.5). Example: log₃(45)=log₃(9·5)=log₃(9)+log₃(5)=2+log₃(5). To combine sums of logs: log(6)+log(7)=log(42). Use to solve equations: log(x)+log(x-3)=1 → log(x(x-3))=1 → x²-3x=10 → x²-3x-10=0 → (x-5)(x+2)=0 → x=5 (reject x=-2 because log is undefined for non-positives). On the Algebra 2 Regents, the product property is one of three core log laws students must apply when expanding, condensing, and solving logarithmic equations. The property derives from the corresponding exponent rule b^a·b^c=b^(a+c).

questions.prompt: Law log_b(M/N)=log_b(M)-log_b(N) that turns a log of a quotient into a difference of logs.
questions.explanation: The quotient property of logs: log_b(M/N)=log_b(M)-log_b(N) (A-SSE.2, F-BF.5). Example: log₂(32/8)=log₂(32)-log₂(8)=5-3=2 (check: log₂(4)=2 ✓). To solve log(x+2)-log(x-1)=1: log((x+2)/(x-1))=1 → (x+2)/(x-1)=10 → x+2=10x-10 → x=12/9=4/3. Check: (4/3+2)/(4/3-1)=(10/3)/(1/3)=10, log(10)=1 ✓. On the Algebra 2 Regents, the quotient property pairs with product and power properties to condense or expand logs as needed; the choice depends on whether the goal is to combine logs (to apply inverse) or split a single log (to evaluate). Derived from b^a/b^c=b^(a-c).

questions.prompt: Curve y=log_b(x) with vertical asymptote x=0, passing through (1,0), and increasing for b>1.
questions.explanation: A logarithmic graph has shape determined by base b (F-IF.7e). For b>1: domain x>0, range all reals, vertical asymptote x=0, x-intercept (1,0), passing through (b,1). For 0<b<1: same domain and range, but decreasing. The graph approaches -∞ as x→0⁺ (for b>1). Transformations apply as usual: y=log(x-2)+3 shifts the parent log right 2 and up 3, with vertical asymptote x=2. On the Algebra 2 Regents, log graphs appear in inverse-function items (graph of exponential and its log inverse) and in modeling slow-growth phenomena. The log graph grows but does so slowly: doubling x adds only a fixed amount to y, not multiplies.

questions.prompt: Equation containing a logarithm of a variable expression, like log_2(x+3)=4.
questions.explanation: A logarithmic equation contains logarithms of variable expressions (A-REI.3). Solve by isolating the log and rewriting in exponential form: log_b(M)=c ↔ M=b^c. Example: log₂(x+3)=4 → x+3=2⁴=16 → x=13. For multiple logs, first apply log laws to combine into one log, then exponentiate: log(x)+log(x+3)=1 → log(x(x+3))=1 → x²+3x=10 → x²+3x-10=0 → x=2 or x=-5; reject x=-5 (log undefined). On the Algebra 2 Regents, every log equation requires a domain check at the end: candidates must produce positive arguments in every original logarithm.

questions.prompt: Equation with a variable in the exponent, like 3^x=27 or 5^(2x-1)=20.
questions.explanation: An exponential equation has the variable as an exponent (A-REI.3). If both sides can be expressed with the same base, equate exponents: 3^x=27=3³ → x=3. Otherwise, take the log of both sides: 5^(2x-1)=20 → log(5^(2x-1))=log(20) → (2x-1)log(5)=log(20) → 2x-1=log(20)/log(5)≈1.861 → x≈1.431. For the natural-base form: e^(2x)=10 → 2x=ln(10) → x=ln(10)/2≈1.151. On the Algebra 2 Regents, exponential equations show up in growth and decay problems where solving for time t requires logarithms. Example: A=Pe^(rt) → t=ln(A/P)/r.

questions.prompt: Formula A=P(1+r/n)^(nt) modeling money growing with periodic compounding.
questions.explanation: Compound interest: A=P(1+r/n)^(nt), where P is principal, r is annual rate (decimal), n is compoundings per year, t is years, A is final amount (F-IF.8b). Example: $1000 at 5% compounded quarterly for 10 years: A=1000(1+0.05/4)^(4·10)=1000(1.0125)^40≈$1643.62. Compare to annual compounding (n=1): A≈$1628.89; quarterly is slightly better. The continuous compounding limit: as n→∞, A→Pe^(rt). On the Algebra 2 Regents, compound interest items test setup (assign variables correctly), evaluation (use calculator), and inverse questions (solve for t using logs). Annual percentage yield (APY) is the effective annual rate after compounding.

questions.prompt: Formula A=Pe^(rt) where money or population grows smoothly without discrete compounding intervals.
questions.explanation: Continuous growth uses A=Pe^(rt), where e≈2.71828 (F-IF.8b). This is the limit of compound interest as n→∞. Example: $5000 at 4% continuously for 8 years: A=5000·e^(0.04·8)=5000·e^0.32≈$6885.64. To find time to reach a target: A/P=e^(rt) → ln(A/P)=rt → t=ln(A/P)/r. Same form models continuously-reproducing populations and continuously-released chemical reactions. On the Algebra 2 Regents, students distinguish compound (discrete) from continuous models by reading 'compounded continuously' in the problem. The constant e was first studied by Jacob Bernoulli analyzing compound interest in 1683 and is named for Leonhard Euler.

questions.prompt: Decay model N(t)=N₀(1/2)^(t/h) where h is the time for a substance to drop to half its initial amount.
questions.explanation: Half-life model: N(t)=N₀(1/2)^(t/h), where N₀ is initial amount, h is half-life (F-IF.8b). Example: Carbon-14 has half-life 5730 years; a sample starting at 100g has after 11,460 years: 100·(1/2)^(11460/5730)=100·(1/2)²=25g. To find age from remaining fraction: 0.25=(1/2)^(t/5730) → t/5730=2 → t=11,460. Equivalent forms use e: N(t)=N₀·e^(-kt) where k=ln(2)/h. On the Algebra 2 Regents, half-life appears in radioactive decay, drug elimination, and cooling problems. The model is exponential decay with a specific 'half' reference point. Doubling time analog: N(t)=N₀·2^(t/d) for growth with doubling time d.

questions.prompt: Time interval for a quantity growing exponentially to multiply by 2, calculated as t=ln(2)/r for continuous growth.
questions.explanation: Doubling time is the period for an exponentially growing quantity to double (F-IF.8b). For continuous growth A=Pe^(rt), set A=2P: 2=e^(rt) → ln 2=rt → t=ln(2)/r. Example: 7% continuous growth gives doubling time ln(2)/0.07≈9.9 years. Rule of 70 (or 72): doubling time ≈ 70/(100r) for small r. Compound version: 2=(1+r)^t → t=log(2)/log(1+r). On the Algebra 2 Regents, doubling time appears in population, investment, and growth modeling. Pairs with half-life for decay: t_half=ln(2)/k. Recognizing the inverse relationship between growth rate and doubling time is a frequent Regents item.

questions.prompt: Technique fitting y=a·b^x to data, found on a calculator's STAT menu under ExpReg.
questions.explanation: Exponential regression fits y=a·b^x (or equivalently y=a·e^(kx)) to bivariate data (S-ID.6, F-LE.5). On the calculator: enter data in lists L1, L2; STAT → CALC → ExpReg L1, L2; calculator returns a and b plus r and r² (correlation and coefficient of determination). Example: bacteria counts at hours 0,1,2,3,4 are 100,180,324,583,1050; ExpReg returns y≈100·(1.80)^x with r²≈0.9999, suggesting an excellent exponential fit. On the Algebra 2 Regents, students often perform regression and then interpret: a=initial value, b=growth factor (b>1 growth, 0<b<1 decay), percent change = 100(b-1)%. Compare exponential to other model types using r² and residual plots.

questions.prompt: Law log_b(M^p)=p·log_b(M) that brings an exponent out front as a multiplier.
questions.explanation: The power property of logs: log_b(M^p)=p·log_b(M) (A-SSE.2, F-BF.5). Example: log(x³)=3·log(x); log₂(16)=log₂(2⁴)=4·log₂(2)=4. To solve 5^x=12: log(5^x)=log(12) → x·log(5)=log(12) → x=log(12)/log(5)≈1.544. The power property is the key tool for solving any exponential equation that can't be reduced to a common base. Combined with product and quotient properties, it lets students expand log(x³y/z) into 3log(x)+log(y)-log(z) for differentiation or analysis. On the Algebra 2 Regents, the power property appears in solving exponential equations and in expanding or condensing logarithmic expressions.

questions.prompt: Line y=k that an exponential graph approaches as x→-∞ for growth or x→+∞ for decay.
questions.explanation: An exponential function y=a·b^x has a horizontal asymptote at y=0 (parent function) or y=k (after vertical shift) (F-IF.7e). For y=2^x: as x→-∞, y→0 (asymptote y=0); as x→+∞, y→∞. For y=2^x+3, asymptote is y=3. For decay y=(1/2)^x: as x→∞, y→0; as x→-∞, y→∞. The asymptote is also the limiting value: in a cooling model T(t)=T_room+(T₀-T_room)e^(-kt), the room temperature T_room is the asymptote. On the Algebra 2 Regents, identifying horizontal asymptotes of exponential graphs is a graph-matching staple, and interpreting the asymptote in context (e.g., 'population stabilizes at X') is a constructed-response task.

questions.prompt: Formula log_b(x)=log_a(x)/log_a(b) used to evaluate logs of any base on a basic calculator.
questions.explanation: The change-of-base formula: log_b(x)=log_a(x)/log_a(b) for any valid base a (F-BF.5). Calculators have keys for log (base 10) and ln (base e) only, so to evaluate log₅(23): log(23)/log(5)≈1.954 or ln(23)/ln(5)≈1.954. The formula is derived by letting y=log_b(x), so b^y=x; taking log_a of both sides: y·log_a(b)=log_a(x), giving y=log_a(x)/log_a(b). On the Algebra 2 Regents, change-of-base is essential for evaluating logarithms of non-standard bases, and for transforming logarithmic equations into a form that can be solved with the calculator's available log functions. Either log or ln on top works.

questions.prompt: Domain of y=log_b(x) requiring the argument inside the logarithm to be positive.
questions.explanation: The domain of y=log_b(x) is x>0 (F-IF.5). For transformed logs y=log_b(g(x)), the domain consists of all x where g(x)>0. Example: y=log(2x-6) has domain 2x-6>0 → x>3, written (3,∞). For y=log(x²-4): x²-4>0 → x²>4 → x<-2 or x>2, written (-∞,-2)∪(2,∞). On the Algebra 2 Regents, students state domain restrictions of logarithmic functions in interval or set-builder notation, and use these restrictions to reject extraneous solutions of logarithmic equations. Whenever a candidate solution makes any log argument non-positive, it must be rejected as extraneous.

questions.prompt: Use of P(t)=P₀(1+r)^t or P(t)=P₀·e^(rt) to project growing populations of people or organisms.
questions.explanation: Population models use exponential functions: discrete P(t)=P₀(1+r)^t or continuous P(t)=P₀·e^(rt) (F-IF.8b, F-LE.2). Example: a town of 50,000 growing at 2.5% annually for 20 years: P(20)=50000(1.025)^20≈81,930. To find when the population reaches 100,000: 100000=50000(1.025)^t → 2=(1.025)^t → t=log(2)/log(1.025)≈28.07 years. Bacteria, viruses, and animal populations follow similar exponential models in their growth phases (before resource limits kick in—a topic for logistic curves). On the Algebra 2 Regents, population modeling shows up in straight-line or pure-exponential phases, sometimes contrasting with logistic data.

questions.prompt: Decay model V(t)=V₀(1-r)^t for assets like cars and electronics losing value over time.
questions.explanation: Depreciation uses exponential decay V(t)=V₀(1-r)^t, where r is the annual depreciation rate (F-IF.8b, F-LE.2). Example: a $30,000 car depreciating 15% annually has value after 5 years: V(5)=30000(0.85)^5≈$13,311. To find when value drops to half: 0.5=(0.85)^t → t=log(0.5)/log(0.85)≈4.265 years. The base (1-r) is the retention factor; with r=0.15 the car retains 85% of value each year. On the Algebra 2 Regents, depreciation modeling appears alongside compound interest as a contrasting case (1-r vs. 1+r). Linear depreciation (subtract fixed amount per year) is sometimes contrasted with exponential depreciation in interpretation questions.

questions.prompt: Decay model N(t)=N₀·e^(-kt) or N(t)=N₀(1/2)^(t/h) for radioactive isotopes losing nuclei.
questions.explanation: Radioactive decay follows N(t)=N₀(1/2)^(t/h) or equivalently N(t)=N₀·e^(-kt) where k=ln(2)/h (F-IF.8b). Half-life h is the time for the substance to reduce by half. Famous examples: Carbon-14 (5730 yr, used in archaeological dating), Iodine-131 (8.02 days, medical thermography), Uranium-238 (4.5 billion yr, age of Earth). To date a sample with 30% of original Carbon-14 remaining: 0.30=(1/2)^(t/5730) → t=5730·log(0.3)/log(0.5)≈9953 years. On the Algebra 2 Regents, radioactive decay problems test exponential function setup, calculator use, and logarithmic solution of time variables.

questions.prompt: Acid-strength scale pH=-log[H⁺] where each unit change is a factor of 10 in hydrogen ion concentration.
questions.explanation: pH=-log₁₀[H⁺] measures acidity on a logarithmic scale (F-BF.5 application). [H⁺] is hydrogen ion concentration in mol/L. pH=7 is neutral (pure water, [H⁺]=10⁻⁷). pH<7 acidic; pH>7 basic. Each pH unit is a factor of 10: lemon juice pH 2 has 100x more H⁺ than tomato juice pH 4. From pH=5.3: [H⁺]=10^(-5.3)≈5.0×10⁻⁶ M. From [H⁺]=2.5×10⁻⁹: pH=-log(2.5×10⁻⁹)≈8.6. On the Algebra 2 Regents, pH is a canonical real-world application of logarithms, illustrating compression of orders-of-magnitude data and giving practice with negative-exponent scientific notation. Similar log scales: Richter (earthquake), decibel (sound).

questions.prompt: Newton's model T(t)=T_room+(T₀-T_room)e^(-kt) describing how hot objects cool to room temperature.
questions.explanation: Newton's Law of Cooling: T(t)=T_room+(T₀-T_room)e^(-kt) (F-IF.8b). T_room is ambient temperature (horizontal asymptote), T₀ is initial object temperature, k is the cooling constant determined by the object/environment. Example: coffee at 200°F in 70°F room with k=0.05/min: T(20)=70+(200-70)e^(-0.05·20)=70+130·e^(-1)≈117.8°F. To find when coffee reaches 100°F: 100=70+130e^(-0.05t) → 30/130=e^(-0.05t) → t=-20·ln(30/130)≈29.4 min. On the Algebra 2 Regents, Newton's cooling problems test exponential modeling with a non-zero horizontal asymptote (the room temperature), solved with natural logarithms.

questions.prompt: Angle measure where one full revolution equals 2π, used as standard in calculus and Algebra 2.
questions.explanation: Radian measure expresses angles as arc length on a unit circle (F-TF.1). One radian is the angle subtending an arc of length 1 on a circle of radius 1. Full circle = 2π rad = 360°. Conversion: degrees × (π/180) = radians; radians × (180/π) = degrees. Common: 30°=π/6, 45°=π/4, 60°=π/3, 90°=π/2, 180°=π, 270°=3π/2, 360°=2π. Why radians? They make trig derivatives simple (d/dx[sin x]=cos x in radians; messy in degrees). Arc length s=rθ requires θ in radians. On the Algebra 2 Regents, students fluently convert and evaluate trig functions in either unit, but radians are standard.

questions.prompt: Circle of radius 1 centered at the origin used to define sine, cosine, and tangent of any angle.
questions.explanation: The unit circle is the circle x²+y²=1 (F-TF.2). For an angle θ measured from the positive x-axis, the terminal point on the unit circle is (cos θ, sin θ). Key values: (1,0) at θ=0; (0,1) at θ=π/2; (-1,0) at θ=π; (0,-1) at θ=3π/2. The 'sweet spots': cos(π/6)=√3/2, sin(π/6)=1/2; cos(π/4)=sin(π/4)=√2/2; cos(π/3)=1/2, sin(π/3)=√3/2. The unit circle visualizes signs by quadrant: Q1 all positive; Q2 sin>0, cos<0; Q3 both negative; Q4 cos>0, sin<0. On the Algebra 2 Regents, the unit circle is the foundational tool for evaluating exact trig values without a calculator.

questions.prompt: Ray from the origin through the point determined by an angle in standard position, ending at the unit circle.
questions.explanation: The terminal side of an angle θ in standard position (vertex at origin, initial side on positive x-axis) is the ray rotated by θ from the initial side (F-TF.2). The terminal side intersects the unit circle at (cos θ, sin θ). For θ=5π/6 (150°), terminal side is in Q2 at (-√3/2, 1/2). Coterminal angles share the same terminal side—they differ by integer multiples of 2π. Reference angles are the acute angle between the terminal side and the x-axis; for 5π/6, the reference angle is π/6 (180°-150°=30°). On the Algebra 2 Regents, identifying which quadrant the terminal side lies in determines signs of trig values.

questions.prompt: Acute angle between an angle's terminal side and the x-axis, used to evaluate trig of any angle.
questions.explanation: A reference angle θ' is the acute (positive) angle between the terminal side of θ and the x-axis (F-TF.2). It is always between 0 and π/2. Computation: Q1: θ'=θ; Q2: θ'=π-θ; Q3: θ'=θ-π; Q4: θ'=2π-θ. Example: for θ=5π/4 (Q3), θ'=5π/4-π=π/4, so sin(5π/4)=-sin(π/4)=-√2/2 (negative because sin is negative in Q3). The reference angle gives the magnitude of the trig value; the quadrant determines the sign. On the Algebra 2 Regents, reference angles are the standard technique for evaluating exact trig values for angles outside Q1 without a calculator.

questions.prompt: Angle sharing the same terminal side as a given angle, found by adding or subtracting multiples of 2π.
questions.explanation: Coterminal angles share a terminal side and differ by an integer multiple of 2π (F-TF.2). To find coterminal angles, add or subtract 2π (or 360° in degrees). Example: θ=11π/3 minus 2π=11π/3-6π/3=5π/3 (one coterminal angle in [0,2π)). For -5π/6: add 2π=-5π/6+12π/6=7π/6 (positive coterminal angle). Coterminal angles have identical trig values: sin(11π/3)=sin(5π/3)=-√3/2. The general expression for all coterminal angles: θ + 2πk for integer k. On the Algebra 2 Regents, finding the standard coterminal angle in [0,2π) is required before computing trig values for any out-of-range angle.

questions.prompt: Function f(θ)=sin(θ) with range [-1,1] and period 2π, equal to the y-coordinate on the unit circle.
questions.explanation: The sine function maps an angle θ to the y-coordinate of its point on the unit circle (F-TF.2). Range [-1,1], period 2π, odd function: sin(-θ)=-sin(θ). Key values: sin(0)=0, sin(π/6)=1/2, sin(π/4)=√2/2, sin(π/3)=√3/2, sin(π/2)=1, sin(π)=0, sin(3π/2)=-1. The graph y=sin(θ) is a wave starting at (0,0), reaching maximum 1 at π/2, returning to 0 at π, minimum -1 at 3π/2, back to 0 at 2π. On the Algebra 2 Regents, sine appears in unit circle evaluation, sinusoidal modeling (tides, daylight hours, body temperature), and inverse-trig items.

questions.prompt: Function f(θ)=cos(θ) with range [-1,1] and period 2π, equal to the x-coordinate on the unit circle.
questions.explanation: The cosine function maps angle θ to the x-coordinate of its unit circle point (F-TF.2). Range [-1,1], period 2π, even function: cos(-θ)=cos(θ). Key values: cos(0)=1, cos(π/6)=√3/2, cos(π/4)=√2/2, cos(π/3)=1/2, cos(π/2)=0, cos(π)=-1, cos(3π/2)=0. The graph y=cos(θ) starts at maximum (0,1), drops to 0 at π/2, minimum -1 at π, back to 0 at 3π/2, max again at 2π. Cosine is a phase-shifted sine: cos(θ)=sin(θ+π/2). On the Algebra 2 Regents, cosine appears in unit-circle evaluation, sinusoidal modeling, and the Pythagorean identity sin²+cos²=1.

questions.prompt: Function f(θ)=tan(θ)=sin(θ)/cos(θ) with vertical asymptotes at odd multiples of π/2 and period π.
questions.explanation: The tangent function is tan(θ)=sin(θ)/cos(θ) (F-TF.2). Range all reals, period π, odd. Vertical asymptotes where cos(θ)=0: θ=π/2, 3π/2, etc. (odd multiples of π/2). Key values: tan(0)=0, tan(π/6)=√3/3, tan(π/4)=1, tan(π/3)=√3. tan(π/2) undefined. The graph y=tan(θ) repeats every π, with branches between consecutive asymptotes. tan(θ)>0 in Q1 and Q3 (where sin and cos have same sign); tan(θ)<0 in Q2 and Q4. On the Algebra 2 Regents, tangent appears in unit circle evaluation, in identities, and in modeling situations where the rate sin/cos has meaning (e.g., slope of a line at angle θ to the x-axis).

questions.prompt: Relationship like csc(θ)=1/sin(θ) or sec(θ)=1/cos(θ) defining the three reciprocal trig functions.
questions.explanation: The reciprocal identities define the three reciprocal trig functions: csc(θ)=1/sin(θ), sec(θ)=1/cos(θ), cot(θ)=1/tan(θ)=cos(θ)/sin(θ) (F-TF.8). Domains exclude zeros of the denominator: csc undefined where sin=0 (multiples of π); sec undefined where cos=0 (odd multiples of π/2); cot undefined where sin=0. Range of csc and sec is (-∞,-1]∪[1,∞) because |sin|, |cos|≤1. Examples: csc(π/2)=1/1=1; sec(π/3)=1/(1/2)=2; cot(π/4)=1/1=1. On the Algebra 2 Regents, reciprocal identities appear in simplifying trig expressions and in solving equations involving sec, csc, or cot. Less common than sin/cos/tan but tested.

questions.prompt: Identity sin²θ+cos²θ=1 derived from the unit circle equation x²+y²=1.
questions.explanation: The Pythagorean identity: sin²θ+cos²θ=1 (F-TF.8). Direct consequence of x²+y²=1 with x=cos θ, y=sin θ. Dividing by cos²θ: tan²θ+1=sec²θ. Dividing by sin²θ: 1+cot²θ=csc²θ. These three Pythagorean identities are the most-used trig identities on the Algebra 2 Regents. Applications: (1) If sin θ=3/5 and θ is in Q1, find cos θ: cos²θ=1-9/25=16/25 → cos θ=4/5 (positive in Q1); (2) Simplify (1-cos²x)/sin x = sin²x/sin x = sin x. The identity does not depend on the unit of θ (works in both radians and degrees) because it follows from the geometric Pythagoras theorem.

questions.prompt: Horizontal length of one complete cycle of a sinusoid, equal to 2π/|b| for y=a·sin(b·x).
questions.explanation: Period is the length of one complete cycle: 2π/|b| for y=sin(bx) or y=cos(bx) (F-TF.5). For y=sin(2x), period is 2π/2=π (compressed). For y=sin(x/3), period is 2π/(1/3)=6π (stretched). Tangent has period π (not 2π), so for y=tan(bx) period is π/|b|. Real-world: tides ~12.4 hr period → b=2π/12.4≈0.507; daylight in temperate latitudes ~365 day period → b=2π/365. On the Algebra 2 Regents, students compute b from a given period (b=2π/period) when writing a sinusoidal equation from a context, and conversely identify period from b in a given equation.

questions.prompt: Horizontal line y=d through the center of a sinusoid, the average of its maximum and minimum.
questions.explanation: The midline of y=a·sin(b(x-c))+d is the horizontal line y=d (F-TF.5). It is the average of the maximum and minimum: midline = (max+min)/2. Example: y=2·sin(x)+5 has midline y=5, max 7, min 3. In context, the midline represents the average value: average tide level, average daylight (12 hours), average body temperature (98.6°F). The vertical shift d moves the entire graph up or down without changing amplitude or period. On the Algebra 2 Regents, midline is one of the four sinusoidal parameters: a (amplitude), b (period), c (phase shift), d (midline). Students compute midline as the average of two extreme values from a context.

questions.prompt: Horizontal translation c of a sinusoid y=a·sin(b(x-c))+d, moving the graph left or right.
questions.explanation: Phase shift is the horizontal translation c in y=a·sin(b(x-c))+d (F-TF.5). Positive c shifts right; negative c shifts left. Be careful with factoring: y=sin(2x-π/3) is y=sin(2(x-π/6)), so the phase shift is π/6 (not π/3). The phase shift moves a feature (like a peak or a midline crossing) to a new x-coordinate. In context: a tide that has its first peak at 3 AM (instead of midnight) has phase shift c=3 hours. On the Algebra 2 Regents, phase shift is often determined by setting the inside of the trig function equal to zero (or to π/2 for cosine-to-sine conversions) and solving for x. Misidentifying the factoring is a common error.

questions.prompt: Number of complete cycles per unit time or distance, equal to 1/period or b/(2π) for sinusoids.
questions.explanation: Frequency is the number of cycles per unit (F-TF.5). For y=a·sin(bx)+d, frequency = b/(2π) = 1/period. Example: y=sin(4πx) has period 2π/(4π)=1/2 and frequency 2 cycles per unit x. Inverse relationship: high frequency ↔ short period; low frequency ↔ long period. In physics, frequency in Hertz (Hz)=cycles/second; angular frequency ω=2π·frequency (in radians/sec). For sound, A above middle C has frequency 440 Hz. On the Algebra 2 Regents, frequency questions test the relationship between b and observable cycle counts in a given time interval; students compute frequency by counting cycles per unit on a graph or by computing 1/period from an equation.

questions.prompt: Equation containing trigonometric functions and unknown angles, like 2 sin(x)=1.
questions.explanation: A trigonometric equation has unknown angles inside trig functions (F-TF.7). Most have infinitely many solutions due to periodicity, but problems usually specify an interval (e.g., 0≤x<2π). Standard solution: (1) isolate the trig function, (2) find reference angle, (3) use unit circle to locate all solutions in given interval. Example: 2sin x=1 → sin x=1/2 → reference angle π/6 → solutions in [0,2π) are π/6 and 5π/6 (both quadrants where sin is positive).
